examples.title: File Locking in C using fcntl
examples.description: Demonstrates how to implement file locking for concurrent access control using POSIX fcntl functions
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates mandatory file locking using fcntl(). The lockFile() function sets a lock on the file, where F_WRLCK requests an exclusive lock. F_SETLKW blocks until lock is acquired, F_SETLK returns immediately if unsuccessful. The entire file is locked (l_len=0 means end-of-file).

examples.title: Readers-Writers Problem Solution
examples.description: Implements a solution to the readers-writers problem using Python threading with locks and semaphores
examples.code:
import threading
import time

class ReadersWritersFile:
    def __init__(self):
        self.readers_count = 0
        self.readers_lock = threading.Lock()  # Protects readers_count
        self.resource_lock = threading.Lock()  # For writers exclusive access
        self.file_content = ""
    
    def read_file(self, reader_id):
        # Reader acquires readers lock first
        self.readers_lock.acquire()
        self.readers_count += 1
        
        if self.readers_count == 1:
            # First reader locks resource (blocks writers)
            self.resource_lock.acquire()
        
        self.readers_lock.release()
        
        # Reading section - can have multiple readers
        print(f"Reader {reader_id} reads: {self.file_content}")
        time.sleep(0.1)
        
        # Reader releases
        self.readers_lock.acquire()
        self.readers_count -= 1
        
        if self.readers_count == 0:
            # Last reader releases resource (allows writers)
            self.resource_lock.release()
        
        self.readers_lock.release()
    
    def write_file(self, writer_id, data):
        # Writer needs exclusive access
        self.resource_lock.acquire()
        
        # Writing section - exclusive
        self.file_content = data
        print(f"Writer {writer_id} writes: {data}")
        time.sleep(0.1)
        
        self.resource_lock.release()

# Usage demonstration
file = ReadersWritersFile()

def reader_thread(reader_id):
    file.read_file(reader_id)

def writer_thread(writer_id):
    file.write_file(writer_id, f"Message from Writer {writer_id}")
examples.explanation: This Python implementation solves the readers-writers problem. Multiple readers can access simultaneously, but writers get exclusive access. The first reader locks the resource (blocking writers), and the last reader releases it. Writers must wait until all readers finish.

examples.title: Deadlock Prevention - Lock Ordering
examples.description: Demonstrates a deadlock prevention technique using a consistent lock ordering policy
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SafeFileAccess(files, operation)
BEGIN
  // Prevent deadlock by enforcing consistent lock ordering
  // Sort files by their unique identifier before acquiring locks
  
  SORTED_FILES := QUICKSORT(files)  // Sort by file ID/path
  
  FOR EACH file IN SORTED_FILES
  BEGIN
    IF operation = "WRITE" THEN
      LOCK_MODE := EXCLUSIVE
    ELSE
      LOCK_MODE := SHARED
    
    ATTEMPT_LOCK(file, LOCK_MODE)
    
    IF LOCK_FAILED THEN
      // Release all previously acquired locks (rollback)
      FOR EACH acquired IN ACQUIRED_LOCKS
        RELEASE_LOCK(acquired)
      
      // Wait and retry
      WAIT(RANDOM_BACKOFF)
      RETURN FAILURE
    
    ADD file TO ACQUIRED_LOCKS
  END
  
  // All locks acquired successfully - proceed with operation
  PERFORM_FILE_OPERATION(files, operation)
  
  // Release all locks
  FOR EACH lock IN ACQUIRED_LOCKS
    RELEASE_LOCK(lock)
  
  RETURN SUCCESS
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode implements deadlock prevention through lock ordering. By always acquiring locks in a consistent sorted order (by file ID), circular wait condition is eliminated. If lock acquisition fails, all previously acquired locks are released (rollback) rather than waiting, preventing deadlock.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting, where n is number of files
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing acquired locks list

examples.title: File Sharing with Java NIO
examples.description: Demonstrates file locking in Java using NIO channels and FileLock for controlled concurrent access
examples.explanation: This Java program demonstrates file locking using NIO (New I/O) FileLock. The lockFile() method with 'shared=true' acquires a shared lock (multiple readers), while 'shared=false' acquires an exclusive lock. tryLock() returns immediately (non-blocking), returning null if unavailable. FileLock.release() releases the lock.
